source.title: Williams Geismar Olefins Plant Reboiler Rupture and Fire

event.incident_type: Catastrophic equipment failure with explosion and fire
actions_taken: Post-incident improvements in process safety management
Implementation of enhanced safety management systems
event.summary: On June 13, 2013, propylene fractionator Reboiler B at the Williams Geismar Olefins Plant catastrophically ruptured and exploded, resulting in a boiling liquid expanding vapor explosion (BLEVE) and fire. The incident killed two Williams employees and injured 167 workers (3 Williams employees and 164 contractors). The reboiler, which had been offline for 16 months, contained liquid propane that had accumulated while isolated from its pressure relief device. When quench water valves were opened to troubleshoot a fouling problem in the operating reboiler, heat was introduced to the blocked-in standby reboiler, causing liquid thermal expansion and rapid overpressurization. The vessel failed at an estimated pressure of 674-1,212 psig, releasing over 30,000 pounds of flammable hydrocarbons. Root causes included deficiencies in Management of Change (MOC), Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR), and Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) programs, as well as inadequate overpressure protection and lack of equipment-specific operating procedures.
recommendations: Strengthen existing safety management systems and adopt additional safety programs
Implement robust Management of Change practices to analyze hazards in the entire process affected by changes
Conduct thorough Pre-Startup Safety Reviews to verify effective implementation of design intent and proper field equipment installation
Develop equipment-specific operating procedures that address hazards of operation
Ensure adequate overpressure protection through pressure relief valves or administrative controls such as car seals
Install instrumentation to detect process fluid within standby equipment
Use more robust isolation methods such as blinds rather than relying solely on block valves
Establish strong organizational process safety culture with management commitment
Implement robust process safety management programs with continual vigilance
Apply hierarchy of controls when evaluating and selecting safeguards

hazards.name: Isolation valves (block valves on reboiler piping)

threats.name: Accumulation of process fluid in standby reboiler
Introduction of heat to isolated reboiler with closed shell-side valves
Liquid thermal expansion in confined space
Loss of overpressure protection due to isolation from relief device
Inadvertent opening of quench water valves on standby reboiler

controls.name: Pressure relief valve on propylene fractionator
controls.lod_basis: Active pressure relief device designed to open and relieve excess pressure

supporting_text: the reboiler could be isolated from its protective pressure relief valve located on top of the propylene fractionator
the shell of Reboiler B from its protective pressure relief valve on the top of the propylene fractionator
the reboiler lacked adequate overpressure protection

controls.name: Block valves (shell-side inlet and outlet)
controls.barrier_role: Isolation of standby reboiler from process
controls.lod_basis: Closed block valves intended to isolate reboiler from process fluid

supporting_text: Large gate valves such as the ones installed on the Williams reboilers are known to leak
the reboiler could be isolated from its protective pressure relief valve located on top of the propylene fractionator
flammable liquid propane accumulated on the shell side of the standby Reboiler B
The propane could have entered the standby reboiler via a mistakenly opened valve, leaking block valve(s), or another unknown mechanism

controls.name: Car seal (administrative lock on block valve)
controls.barrier_role: Prevent inadvertent opening of isolation valve
controls.lod_basis: Mechanical device that physically locks valve in open or closed position

supporting_text: The MOC reviewers indicated that the valves did not have to be car sealed open
A car seal is a mechanical device that physically locks a valve in the open or closed position to prevent manipulation by an unauthorized person
the option of using a car seal was the only specified overpressure protection method on the MOC checklist

controls.name: Management of Change (MOC) process
controls.barrier_role: Identify and control hazards introduced by process changes
controls.lod_basis: Regulatory requirement and industry good practice to review changes before implementation

supporting_text: Williams did not perform an MOC before installing and commissioning the new block valves on the reboilers
the Williams 2001 MOC review did not identify the significant overpressure hazard introduced by the valves
The MOC reviewers indicated that the valves did not have to be car sealed open
The MOC reviewers incorrectly indicated that existing operating procedures were adequate to account for the new valves
The MOC reviewers improperly indicated that the change did not require a Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)

controls.name: Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)
controls.lod_basis: Regulatory requirement and industry good practice for systematic hazard identification

supporting_text: Williams did not adequately implement action items developed during Process Hazard Analyses (PHAs)
The MOC reviewers improperly indicated that the change did not require a Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)
Williams did not perform a hazard analysis and develop a procedure prior to the operations activities conducted on the day of the incident

controls.name: Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)
controls.barrier_role: Verify effective implementation of design intent and proper field equipment installation
controls.lod_basis: Regulatory requirement to ensure equipment is properly installed and procedures are adequate

supporting_text: Williams did not perform adequate Management of Change (MOC) or Pre-Startup Safety Reviews (PSSRs) for two significant process changes involving the propylene fractionator reboilers
Following the installation of the propylene fractionator Reboiler A and Reboiler B valves, Williams performed a Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR) as required by process safety management regulations

controls.name: Operating procedures for reboiler startup
controls.barrier_role: Provide step-by-step guidance for safe reboiler operation
controls.lod_basis: Regulatory requirement and industry practice to have written procedures for process operations

supporting_text: Williams Geismar had relied on its generic procedure, last revised in 1996, to start up any reboiler within the entire facility

Williams considered this generic procedure applicable to start up the propylene fractionator Reboiler B; however, Williams' generic procedure was based on the assumption that all reboilers had the process fluid on the tube side of the reboiler

A robust, equipment-specific procedure detailing the steps to switch the propylene fractionator reboilers should communicate the importance of opening the process valves (cold side) before opening the quench water valves (hot side)

controls.name: Instrumentation to detect process fluid in standby equipment
controls.barrier_role: Alert operators to presence of process fluid in offline reboiler
controls.lod_basis: Pressure gauge or level indicator to monitor standby vessel condition

supporting_text: Williams had not installed instrumentation to detect process fluid within the reboiler
Reboiler B did not have a pressure gauge installed on its shell to allow for periodic monitoring
A pressure gauge could have alerted the operations supervisor that the Reboiler B shell was at a pressure of at least 124 psig

controls.name: Blind isolation (mechanical isolation plate)
controls.barrier_role: Positive isolation of vessel from process
controls.lod_basis: Metal plate inserted between flanges to ensure positive isolation

supporting_text: More robust isolation methods, such as inserting a blind, can better protect offline equipment from accumulation of process fluid
A blind is a metal plate inserted between flanges to ensure positive isolation of a vessel from the process

controls.name: Hazard analysis and procedure development for nonroutine operations
controls.barrier_role: Identify hazards and develop safe work procedures for nonroutine activities
controls.lod_basis: Industry good practice to analyze hazards before conducting nonroutine operational activities

supporting_text: Williams did not perform a hazard analysis and develop a procedure prior to the operations activities conducted on the day of the incident
Williams personnel were performing this type of nonroutine operational activity when the incident occurred on June 13, 2013

controls.name: Process safety culture and management commitment
controls.barrier_role: Foster organizational values and behaviors that prioritize process safety
controls.lod_basis: Organizational commitment to implementing safety management programs effectively

supporting_text: Weaknesses in these programs resulted from a culture at the facility that did not foster and support strong process safety performance

The MOC was an after-the-fact activity for Williams to address a regulatory requirement rather than an effective tool used to identify and control new process hazards prior to installing the new equipment
process safety management program weaknesses at the Williams Geismar facility during the 12 years leading to the incident caused the reboiler to be unprotected from overpressure

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
